I think he has it in him. It's a capacity of his. So moving on, so this is how the next 60 minutes will go. Yeah, I'm actually gonna introduce a brand new theme plate in this session. So you already have a bunch of them inside 10X launches, six free emails if you have that, but yeah, why not add another one to the mix it's one we've used before and one I've wanted to teach. So yeah, we're gonna do it. We're gonna talk about why we use it, when to use it, how to use it and then you're going to see an extremely meta example of how we've used it so that you can take it and do your own thing with it. So, and following that Q&A. So once again, give as much detail as possible if this is your first Q&A office hours with us. Yeah, just be ready to give some kind of like clarifying answers, you know, if we push back just asking for more detail. And yeah, don't be afraid to just drill deeper and poke at our brains until you get what you need to take action on it. So with all that said, we are gonna start off talking about FOMO, the FOMO is strong with this one. So yeah, there's a lot of memes here. There's a lot of Star Wars and Winnie the Pooh memes. I apologize for that. It's probably just because I didn't have a childhood or something, so I was too busy stealing trophies, but the FOMO is a big thing in marketing and we've spoken about it before and it's essentially the fear of missing out, but here are the two big points I want to make about it. It can't be something we reserve just for our prospects. You know, if you're gonna ask them to feel it, you've gotta meet them halfway and feel it yourself, you know, and when you do that, you can actually elicit your prospects empathy and make them feel safer in reciprocating and really feeling their own fear of missing out. So the way I really kind of bring this up, you know, when I'm writing those emails, I want to tap into what I'm most afraid about if they miss out. You know, if you're genuinely creating and offering something that you know your prospects need and could change their lives in whatever way that is and however that looks, there should be a very genuine fear you have and then missing out and it is totally cool and okay and unselfish for you to share that. In fact, when you share that, they feel more comfortable tapping into that and it's more of a balanced feeling. So that is the first point about BOMO. It's not just something they should feel it's something you should feel for them when you sense their opportunity to buy your product and get that result is starting to slip. And the other point to this is that people don't really care what you know until they know you care. And someone once said that, I don't know who it was. So I'm gonna like give universal credit to that because I definitely picked that up from somewhere. But what I'm trying to get at here is it's up to you as the marketer to make the first move to feel that feeling which signals that it's okay for them. And the more you stack your genuine reasons for caring, the less it feels like this push and pull tussle for their hard earned dollars. So the more safe the safer they feel and just kind of expressing that themselves. So the other way I wanna kind of frame this is, we see so many of these promotions and launches where in the closing days, the marketer will make it sound like everyone already bought and they're having so much fun on the other side and it's a big party on that other side. So that's what I call the we're about to have so much fun without you marketer. And it kind of looks like this, this Johnny Depp and Fieran Loathing. And I mean, I don't like this guy. Nobody likes to feel like somebody is intentionally making them feel jealous about something they're missing out on. So I want us to move away from being this guy that we're about to have so much fun without you marketer and become more of the Rose Telling Jack, I'll never let you go marketer, except be better than Rose. Cause I think she actually let Leo go at the end of that. So be better than Rose, but like the same emotion there. So like you're not going to that party or you're not ready to go to that party without your prospect. It is so important for them to come along that you actually don't feel good closing the card or going to the party or being on the other side without them. You really want to hang on, you really want to show them why you're afraid of them missing it on this big thing. And that is actually eating away at you as well. So that's what I challenge you to be. I challenge you to be the Rose Telling Jack, I'll never let you go marketer. So with all that said, okay, Ra, can we please make this practical now? And I am glad you asked. This is the bonus theme plate. It is called the Piglet. And you know, Piglet is this, I think Piglet was the anxious one, right? If anyone could confirm or deny it's been a while since I've watched. Yes. Yes. So Piglet is the anxious one. I really kind of want you and this email to channel what makes you anxious or nervous about your prospect missing out. You know, that is how you really kind of like channel your own fear. You know, what are they going to miss out on? And really kind of show your intentionality around them, buying what it is you've already declared they need. So this is why we use it. So the first reason is the reverse FOMO for all the reasons that we already spoke about just to show your prospect why it matters to you that they take that action. And then there's also this thing called mirror neurons. It's this like very sciencey neuroscience thing. But essentially by leading with our own concern and kind of demonstrating that behavior around the fear, our prospects will feel more likely to feel good getting in touch with their own. So they can mirror our own anxiety or nervousness or fear out of empathy. Instead of us just kind of like being in this like cool, we're about to party and close the gate and like just telling them to be nervous. It's more likely that they're going to get in tune with the fear of missing out if we first show that there is our fear of them missing out. And finally, it's just kind of this win-win. You know, the conversion becomes more of an opportunity to solve one another's problems, which is very satisfying. Instead of us convincing them to do something which is very kind of like one way, this becomes more of a collaboration, something that they could do to ease our struggle and we could do something to ease there. So win-wins feel good. I think Steven Covey said a lot about that and seven habits of highly effective Winnie the Pooh characters or something like that. So those are the three big reasons why we use it and where do we use it? So you could use this in two places. The first place would be the last day to essentially sign up for anything, you know, whether that's a free webinar, a workshop, a series, a challenge, any kind of like free lead magnet you have on like the front end of your funnel. This could be a place to use it or which you're gonna see in a second, it could be the last day of an offer. And I usually like to have this one as the first email on the last day of a promotion. So. Right. Yes. This is a quick question here, sorry. Bridget asks, can you use FOMO when offering a free product or service? I think can you use something like this when offering a free product or service? Yeah, absolutely. Because I mean, that is still a conversion. There's a reason that you are making that sell. So pretty much anytime you are asking your prospect to take a milestone action, whether it's downloading something, signing up for a free webinar, this is something you could use. Cool. Thanks. Cool. Awesome. Before we get into kind of like the template itself, are there any other questions about what we've covered so far? There's nothing right now. Cool. Nothing's coming in. Yeah, I think we're good. All right. So this is the template. You're essentially gonna open out by just kind of declaring your anxiety. I'm nervous, I'm afraid. However you wanna wear that. And then just kind of add some personal details to build intimacy. And you're gonna see how Joe did that in a second actually. But essentially what this does is if you're going to, if you're gonna ask your audience to believe this very human emotion that you're expressing and actually buy into it, you need to be very human. And one way to do that is to just be intimate in the details you reveal. And that doesn't mean revealing intimate moments about your life. There's a very big difference there. What it means is just, if you're talking about a friend, name them. What's their name? If you're talking about places you go, name the place. So it just means being very hyper specific about those elements of your life that you're bringing in. And you're gonna see how we did that in just a second as well. Why your concern? So this is really the meat of the emails. So you're afraid of them missing out because, and one of them is that crunchy moment of highest tension which they'll continue to experience. That is a reason for you to be nervous and them not taking action. The second one could be more of like an emotional and internal moment of highest tension which they'll continue to experience. The third one would be contrasting the two possible outcomes. You're gonna, I'm nervous because you're gonna continue to experience X when you can be experiencing Y. And reason four, I like this one. It denies you of your big Y or your company's mission. So it really kind of brings once again that personal aspect to it in a very genuine way. Like you are in this business, you are creating and selling this product for a very specific Y. And them not getting involved in that kind of deprives you of that reason. So don't be afraid to be vulnerable and share that. Yes, it is about your prospect but this is an opportunity to actually make it a little bit more about you while reflecting why it matters to them. And then before we get into the close, we'll stack the most logical and arguable no brainer reasons to buy. These are probably gonna be things that you've spoken about earlier on in your funnel. And then that confident final pitch of why your prospect needs what you have. So like sum it up. I mean, if you're selling this thing either on behalf of your client or you are the creator, I mean, you should genuinely believe they need what you have. And don't be afraid to state that with total confidence before making the close. And then I like the PS here to be a proof element to really balance out all those more emotional appeals and all those kind of like more feely aspects of it, if you will. So if you do have a testimonial, a case study, something that can really demonstrate evidence of someone who did buy a product and got those results, that would be a really great way to just kind of balance it out on that final close. So that is the template. I'm gonna take a deep breath. Okay. Joseph Cohen asked, the client I'm writing for has a blog slash voice that is very logical and almost never empathizes with anyone. Well, I'm not incapable of writing empathetic or empathic copy. I'm a bit worried that this will bring up alarm bells with the audience. I'm not sure how to best reconcile this thoughts. So voice issues. Hmm, voice issues. Yes. So I mean, if I could ask, what industry is this or who is the target audience? Oh, here, Joseph posted something below. So maybe this will help. Oh, health he says. Health. Yeah. Health. Okay. I mean, the next question I would ask is kind of like, how are those current emails in that voice performing? Like, is he in a really, really good groove and people are very responsive to that more authoritative? I'm guessing it's more authoritative and more dry tone and more professional. He gives an example. I'll read it out for you, right? He said, for example, I wrote the sales copy that started with negative hormonal effects of insomnia and someone who wasn't a blog reader, I assume this was someone who got the email, said or I assume, okay, said that she would have burst it. She would burst into tears if she read this when she suffered from insomnia. She wanted a more emotionally empathetic, in this case, sales copy or emails. Yeah. I mean, I would really encourage you to just take it up a notch. You don't always have to overhaul someone's voice overnight, but just try to take it up a notch, especially in health where people want to have that space to really feel heard scene around what they're going through. Yeah. I mean, if he's afraid of going too far on that, I mean, you could use this template as an example. I mean, maybe even saying I'm worried is a little too strong and too on the other side. Like if you're gonna, I don't know, what do you think, Joe? Well, I think like this is a hard thing for people when they're talking about voice a lot of the time is this sense that you find your voice, but most of the copywriting that works well, it's really the audience's voice on the page, right? So it's not about how the blog sounds or how you want it to sound. It's like, how do they need it to sound to pay attention? It has to sound like they sound. So if it's a dry voice that you've been using and that's not actually how the people that are reading it speak or think or communicate, then that's cause alone to move it differently. But at the same time, it doesn't mean you go far. You have to find this subtle, they shouldn't hear the voice. It should just be like talking, like it's not screeching. Like it's like a real voice. I don't hear your voice when you're talking, but I know your voice. You're going to talk in certain ways, same with me, right? And it's not anything that's so exaggerated that you notice it because that gets annoying once you start noticing someone's voice. It's like the screechy thing, like that secretary on Twin Peaks. I was rewatching Twin Peaks. That secretary's voice where she's so like, ah, you want her to stop talking. And that's the thing with trying hard with voice in copywriting too, is as soon as people notice it, it's already gone too far. So even if that noticing it is the boring dull lack of voice, that's still something that's being noticed too. And that's going too far in the other direction. So it's tricky and it's hard to tell your client that. For sure. I think the low hanging fruit here, Joseph, is like just being hyper specific in describing how that tension, how that pain shows up in their life like Joe does in those like four reasons over there. That's one of, I mean, the best things I see when Joe writes an email is really being super, super specific. Like it's not like you get stomach cramps. It's you have to excuse yourself from the Monday morning meeting and slide out the door because you couldn't digest that cappuccino. That's all you. That's totally Mr. I completely right. Where like I'm so pulled in, it's the T-Rex thing. The thing you said about the lizard. The lizard anyway. But these like really specific moments. But again, and that's a moment, right? Like that's not throughout. If you were always talking in these crazy, in these really hyper specific ways, every line line by line, that would be exhausting to read too. So just further to your point. Yeah. Does that help? Joe, let us know if that helps. So it's a good discussion about voice, but it's always like more of a discussion than it is anything else. You have to arrive at the voice and just make sure that you're taking everything into consideration when you're trying to get there. Okay, Sarah Jones asks, I have a question. She says, do you feel it's worth it to test webinar funnels against MVF for new subscribers? I think they really like the emails, but I'm not sure if I'm just missing out on a whole new level. I know I can't know for sure unless I test, but in general are there stats to support the webinar being more effective? I've never actually tested a funnel with a webinar or without a webinar. Have you Joe? Nope. No. No, I've tried webinars without, or funnels without webinars, funnels with webinars, and I'll only do them with webinars because they in my experience perform better, but it's not a proper A-B test. Yeah, so we don't actually have stats to support that. You know, I have done an MVF for a coaching funnel, to get applications for a consulting package at the end, and those work if you do this as well. And the reason I do that is, I mean, just speed of rollout. If you're really kind of like, I'd rather a client or even myself get something out quick and actually have it out there, than delay for months trying to get the perfect webinar funnel. So if it is a matter of speed of implementation, MVF has worked well to definitely get applications for coaching as well as, I think we've used it on a few evergreen programs and courses as well. So it does have its use, I've never tested it one against the other. If you do have bandwidth and resources and time to do a webinar funnel, my recommendation is to go with that. The MVF really is there to just have like zero excuse to not rolling something out, because most people never deploy their funnels and that just costs them money and time and isn't for anybody. So yeah. All right, good stuff, Rai. Okay. Chris asks, curious whether the worry theme was something that you, Rai, really noticed about Joe. That's interesting. Is Joe a worrier? No, we are totally not worriers, are we Joe? I mean, I- What do you say? I mean- What's the saying when you worry, you feel the pain twice or something like that? There is a saying, yeah. It goes a lot, look it up, Google that. To be honest, the worry isn't this like, persistent, like all pervading thing that we feel, but when we write and we're really kind of in tune with the audience we're writing for, that's where we become like super in touch with it. So we don't go around like making Tim Hortons runs because we can't have a life. So that isn't the thing. It's not a bad idea. Chris is just clarifying here, a question was more about, was it something you noticed and therefore wrote about? Like it was the trigger or the inspiration for the email or is the email really just based in like something that you've otherwise seen about messaging and conversion? No, it's definitely something that I notice, I feel when I'm writing. So I don't carry around that worry all the time, but when I'm writing for that audience, I do notice. Like how do I feel right now? And I'm like, I actually feel anxious right now. I feel nervous and then I kind of tap into that. And it's like, yeah, I really do believe that this person reading this needs what we are selling here. There is this opportunity that is slipping by. So that is a very genuine anxiety I do feel when I write. Yeah. Yeah, and you and just for everybody, Rai has talked like with my mastermind and other groups I know about the deep empathy that he feels when writing, which I think I know goes deeper than anything I feel when I'm writing copy, like getting to a state of feeling really like emotional about it, right? Like to the point of discomfort. Uh-huh. I'm laughing because I did the 47 questions workbook with a client yesterday actually live here in Denver. And it was the first time like doing it live face to face with somebody. And we actually had to like stop halfway through because I made him too angry. Like I really like got him to like punch a hole in the wall angry, like just talking about what breaks his heart about like his industry and all that. Like, yeah. So warning to anybody who uses those 47 questions like live, like pay attention to like the coloration of your clients face. Like if it's turning Hulk like green, like just just break for five minutes. Wow. But it actually ended up being amazing. Like out of that two hour conversation, I had like the outlines for 10 emails essentially and really the language that ended up being the subject lines for those. So, so yeah, go there. Wow. Yeah. Yeah. It's really saying something. Okay, cool. Sorry, Joe Joseph asked, what do you think the most important email in a launch sequences? Oh wow. This is, I would totally take like the cheap way out and say all of them. What do I think? Okay, so it does depend, right? Like if you are doing a webinar launch and you have really good conversions on the webinar, the most important is probably gonna be the webinar invite to get your list onto there. If you're doing an MVF minimum viable funnel, I mean, the one that's really gonna pull the most weight is probably, I mean, I would say the FAQ, you know, on the like 48 to 24 hours before or one of the closing emails on the last day, but it's hard to single one out. Yeah. Yeah. It's a hard question to choose one. Who's your favorite child? Yeah. Kind of thing, right? Mm-hmm. It's my favorite cat. The one who wants, the one who won all the real trophies. I don't know. Yeah. The one who doesn't bring on participation medals. No idea. Is there something wrong with those? Is there something wrong? If only they were medals. They were like little paper, little, well, I'm from a different time than you are. Maybe yours were medals. Yours were really fancy. We had medals, yes. You did. Ours was just like a ribbon. Yeah, a ribbon. Whatever. They went in the wash and then you threw them out. Yeah. And not recycle at the time, of course, because, okay. So, let's see, Jesse asks, guys, would you vary the emotions when talking to an audience of mostly men versus when talking to mostly women? Or the words you use, men and women tend to communicate their emotions differently, right? Or are things changing in the business world due to coaching and the emotional intelligence trend? That is an amazing question. Do you want to take first stab or should I take? No, you go first. You look like you have thoughts. I don't know. Many. Many. Okay, so I wouldn't make the overgeneralization of men. I mean, I think be more in tune with kind of the type of men you're communicating with. You know, you do have a gauge of, you know, what they signed up, first of all. If you're talking to men who signed up for something in like the self-development sphere or the men being more vulnerable sphere, whatever that sphere happens to be, you can act accordingly. But another thought there is like, just because a man has trouble expressing that vulnerability and the emotions doesn't mean he won't appreciate the reflection of it, you know, or even the invitation to access that. So in any case, I don't see a benefit in being closed off as the marketer, you know, and limiting the opportunity for them to see that reflection. So there just doesn't seem to be much, anything to be had there by not expressing emotion or even like a depth of it as a marketer. Yeah, yeah, agreed. I mean, it's definitely, I wouldn't say it's about men versus women because I think a lot of people don't fit in, like most people don't fit into any certain mold anyway. And we all kind of know that. I wrote for a company called Machine-O-Matic years ago. You can imagine that was a pretty, our audience was largely guys, largely over 40. And it didn't mean that I adjusted the way we spoke to like pull emotion out or something or feel like, oh, they're gonna be closed off. It was still about whatever that emotion was that was gonna move them. But it doesn't mean like we're moving people to sob all the time or anything like that. Cause I know a lot of women wouldn't, what? So it's, yeah, it's just, I wouldn't make decisions based on, oh, this is a male audience or this is a female audience. I might do something more based on if it's an audience of a certain age that consumes content in different ways. Maybe I would adjust accordingly and feel like there's more opportunities to pull different levers based on what might be true based on the generation they might have grown up in or the tech they're used to using or the references that they're going to get, things like that. But yeah, not for emotion. I personally haven't, and wouldn't. And like, right, knowing you, right? Like you're a dude and you're very emotionally available. So I don't, Lance is emotionally available. Everybody, all the guys I know are actually quite emotionally available. And the women I would say are along the same lines too. So from my perspective, I wouldn't feel any drive at least to change it. Yeah. Cool, okay, thanks, Jesse. Good question. Andrea asked, you have, oh, this is interesting, kind of a glossary. Do you have a list of the various acronyms you use? I know some of them, but not all. There are lots of them. Yeah, so, I mean, I never thought I was cool enough to have my very own glossary. You are. Yeah, so we will put that together. Yes, I'm guessing you're talking about things I'm using like MVF, MOHT, QVC, blah, blah, blah. Yeah, all those things. So yeah, we will put that together for you, for sure. I heard Pofu at a conference recently. So there was- Key to a point. And I was like, it's bad enough to have Pofu, MOHT, MOHT, MOHT, but as soon as you get Pofu in there, I'm done. I have to leave the conversation. It was like- You're off the Pofu train. Yeah. Something about post-purchase, a funnel, or something like that. Oh my God. It made sense when you said it, but I was just, I was dreading it the whole time. After you said, Pofu, I was like, stop, please stop. I can't hear it anymore. Yeah, it's awful. Yes, there are some bad things. That is what, Pofu is one day gonna be like an add-on. You could put in a salad. It's gonna be like- It sounds like, yeah, like I'd get it at Whole Foods or something. Yeah, like after there's the whole like save the soy movement, which is gonna happen, because I'm sure people care about saving soy. And then- Sure. The P will be the tofu substitute source. Yeah, exactly. Whatever that is. It's a market. Cool, so yes, glossary on the way. Glossary, okay, so I don't say any more questions. I see people wanna talk more around targeting men, et cetera. Anything else here? I'm not seeing any others. Suzanne mentioned something, I think, just to follow up on her other MBF question. Kay just posted, how do you decide on length of time and spacing for a launch sequence with an eye on creating a sense of urgency? Length of time and spacing. So I do like to have most launches within, you know, a seven to 14 day range. I feel like any longer, and sometimes that journey gets a little exhausting, but you do see longer journeys with like three-part video series and giving enough days in between for people to absorb the material. And especially, that's especially useful if you do have joint venture partners and affiliates because they need time to get their audiences involved as well. So there are exceptions where a longer journey would make sense, but generally speaking, I like a good seven to 14 day window, usually hovering more around like 10 days, which is typically what you'll see on evergreen webinar launches if you see that. You know, the time between registration and often for the webinar and when it closes is usually about seven to 10 days. That usually feels about right spacing for it. So if we are gonna do something in that like seven to 10 day range, I'm not giving really any space between those communications. You're really creating this focused ecosystem where you wanna stay top of mind throughout that intensive period. So that normally looks daily, depending on, you know, if you have certain kind of like behavioral triggers in there and maybe they already kind of watched a webinar. So they're not gonna get like the replay the next day, but they're gonna get follow ups, but generally daily in a seven to 10 day window. Cool, yeah. And on copy hackers, there is a 21 day launch guide that you can download just a free thing that we put together for 10 X emails. So if you wanna go grab that, that can be a quick, helpful thing. Cool, okay, any other questions guys? We're here at, oh, yep, just like that. Jesse says, when you're doing a launch with a lot of emails and do you block other emails like we newsletters? Yeah, so I'm gonna give credit to my friend, Greg Hickman here of system.leand. He created this tag called do not disturb, which he puts on everybody when they're going through a campaign so that they're not getting messages from the same author that don't serve the narrative or the journey that they're building. So yes, typically that is a good practice. Cool, I just chatted that link out to system.le. So that's cool, yeah, we definitely, we pause all of our newsletters or drip campaigns that are not related to the launch when we are in launch mode. That's right. Cool, okay, great, guys, any other questions? Are you good with launching? Are you, what do you do? Suzanne asks, what do you do after the launch is over with the emails? So like what you do with the emails you've written? Like, I don't know, like is it about storing them or like do you continue? Okay, okay, so you don't mean like throwing them up in the air like it's the last day of school? We don't need these anymore. Like do you continue? Okay, so obviously at the end of the launch, people are on different paths. You have the people who bought and purchased who could be put on a, what would be called like a stick campaign or an engagement campaign who makes sure they're interacting with whatever they just bought. In e-commerce, obviously you have like different emails which would be kind of like to review it or even like complimentary products. So things of that nature. People who didn't buy which will always represent like the majority of your subscribers. I mean, you have options here. You know, one option that we've done is, you know, you can send out surveys asking them why they didn't buy, really kind of see what's blocking them there and really get that feedback. And that feedback may or may not trigger an alternate offer. Like if you saw they needed something else that you just so happen to have, that could be a segue into a much shorter launch window because they've already gone through something bigger but a three or four day down sell or even an up sell. You can even have that triggered as an up sell. You know, if somebody didn't buy your course because they don't have time to do it themselves and they tell you that the up sell could be done for them. You know, if you're an agency which is a much more high ticket thing. So that would be my recommendation is really gauge that feedback, have those options, try to see why they didn't buy and create those options based on things you may be able to down sell or up sell them to later. Cool, love, excellent. Okay, Joseph asked, do you usually split test the emails with a small fraction of the list before sending it out? We've had a few emails that were people the wrong way. Hmm, what do you say, Joe? No, so no, we segment and where it makes sense to do testing, we do, but it's generally for the subject line, not for other things. Split testing for emails, by the way, ConvertKit now has subject line testing just for everybody who was like, where's the testing in this tool? It's in there now. But it's hard with split testing when you're in, especially because we do short launch windows, right? Like it's open for five, the cart open for five, five and a half days. And I mean, split testing, you have to send out the email, wait four hours to find the winner and then send it out to everybody else. So getting timing right can be tough there. So split testing isn't brilliant in emails and for us, as long as we're segmenting well and feeling good about that, I know in this case, Joseph said, you know, a few emails that rub people the wrong way. We've sent those before and we live with it. I mean, the idea of trying to get your copy to a place where the average person is going to be okay with it. I wouldn't worry too much about that. I let those emails come in and then the other ones, the way we know it's working is people, other people bought, lots of people bought, more than we expected bought. Right, what are your thoughts? So I've known people who have had like something written on their keyboard saying, never be boring. I have that and I also have never be benign. Like I do want a friction, you know? And friction drives action, right? So it's got to rub them into motion. Sometimes that motion has them running the other way or sometimes it has them confronting crap that they don't want to confront and that is what is rubbing them the wrong way. But essentially just never be benign with what I'm sending doesn't have a chance to create some sort of response that I don't feel I'm doing my job. And yes, people are gonna have adverse reactions to that as well, but obviously we aim for that to be the minority and the exception. But yeah. And we wrote about this on Copy Hacker's, right? About that email that we sent out for a 10x landing pages? Yes, it was the power of a polarizing email. Yes. That very sarcastic landing page writing. It's, I think that landing page like had really good movie and music taste. I can't remember. It did. But it was a very polarizing email. It made you notice it and some people don't want you to make them feel anything and they'll get mad at you for it. For sure. And I mean granted, like some of the like negative reactions on that were things that probably didn't have to be there. We'd noticed it. Like one thing I think I mentioned like a redheaded stepchild because like I had a lot of red in my beard and I felt like I can make that joke. And then he got people super on the defensive there. So obviously- Which is like, because Lance is a redhead. So I felt okay sending it, right? Yeah. It's like, yeah, okay. We're familiar with that sentiment. Yeah. So sometimes like, I mean, if you do want to kind of avoid some of those things you can, you know, that didn't really further the email. But at the same time, I don't know. Maybe it did. Yeah. So, yeah. Never be benign. I love it. Okay. Yeah. Andrea asked, can you repeat the same email funnel sequence a few months later? Like two times a year, for example. Hmm. So ideally you would not send the same email sequence to people who saw it before because if it didn't move them to action last time, then chances are it's not going to move them to action this time unless their circumstances have so drastically changed that they're a perfect fit for what you're doing. Where you would reuse the same emails so that you do get more mileage out of it is if you are doing an evergreen sequence and you have new subscribers coming into that funnel who haven't seen those emails before, then yes. I would definitely say look at the best performing emails of that sequence. Keep those. Use them for people who haven't seen them before. And yeah, why not? Yeah, cool. Yeah. Joseph was just asking further to the testing question. What if you have other tools that allow it? So we've, I think we've addressed that. Then one more comment. Joseph, you're being very vocal. I love it. Sometimes the more emotionally provoking you are, the more likely you are to piss people off. Exactly, right? Cool. So you run a risk when you make people feel something. But the bigger risk is them feeling nothing at all. So there's that. Okay, 1150 votes. That's something like a song lyric, like written from like 2004. It was, that was Hoony. I was totally quoting, wait Hoony's the 90s. Come up with something from 2004. What's a band from 2004? Like they have a number in their name, like a SUM41 or? Blink 182 or SUM41. Some sort of number, right? That's how you know they were from the 2000s. That's a band, yeah. All right, cool. Right. Thanks for that awesome training today. People are saying they loved it. I don't know why you'd put a sales page on your site. It can be much better when you send emails to them and nurture people towards the sales page. Raw, your thoughts on that? No, I mean, I've never really had a sales page live on a site either, so I can't really say it, but. You agree. Yeah. Yeah. Yeah, okay, cool. All right, thanks. I'll email you the page, yeah. Cool.
